1: oikonomos — manager, steward — oikonomos (manager, steward of a household — from oikos = house + nomos = law/management; the one who manages the house's affairs; from which "economy") is the key term for the entire parable. The manager does not own the property but is entrusted to administer it; his accountability to the master is the parable's framing tension. Diaskorpizōn ta hyparchonta autou — squandering his possessions — the same diaskorpizō (scatter, squander) used for the prodigal son's waste (15:13); the parable is deliberately set after the prodigal and echoes its vocabulary.
2: apodos ton logon tēs oikonomias sou — give an account of your management — logon tēs oikonomias (account of the stewardship — logos = word, account, reckoning; oikonomia = management, stewardship; from which "economy") is the formal audit demand. Ouketi dunasai oikonomein — you can no longer be manager — the dismissal is announced before the account is given, based on the accusation alone. The manager's crisis is not whether the accusation is true but what to do now that his position is ending: the parable's cleverness lies in the manager's resourceful response to irreversible termination.
3: ti poiēsō, hoti ho kyrios mou aphaireitai tēn oikonomian ap emou — what shall I do, since my master is taking away the management from me — the interior monologue (eipen en heautō = said to himself; also 12:17 for the rich fool; 15:17 for the prodigal) is Luke's device for revealing the character's reasoning process. Skaptein ouk ischuō, epaitein aischunomai — I cannot dig, I am ashamed to beg — skaptō (dig — manual labor; the manager is a white-collar figure, not a field-worker) + epaitō (beg — from epi- + aitō = ask for; beg insistently); the two rejected options frame his social position: above manual labor by profession but too proud for begging.
4: egnōn ti poiēsō — I know what I will do — the manager's decision arrives as a flash of insight (egnōn = I know/came to know — aorist of ginōskō; the moment of recognition). Hina hotan metastathō tēs oikonomias dexōntai me eis tous oikous autōn — so that when I am removed from the stewardship they will receive me into their homes — metastathō (when I am removed — aorist passive subjunctive of methistēmi = remove, displace; also Acts 13:22 for removing Saul) + dechomai eis oikous (receive into homes — the hospitality word): by generating debtor-gratitude before dismissal, he secures future hospitality.
5: proskalesmenos hena hekaston tōn chreopheiletōn tou kyriou heautou — calling in each one of his master's debtors — chreopheiletēs (debtor — from chreos = debt + opheiletēs = one who owes; the compound = one who owes a debt; hapax in Luke, also 7:41 in the two-debtors parable). The manager calls each debtor privately (hena hekaston = each one, one by one) — the process is individual and confidential, suggesting the manager wants to minimize visibility of his scheme. Posō opheileis tō kyriō mou — how much do you owe my master — the inquiry is the setup for the reduction.
6: hekaton batous elaiou... pentēkonta — a hundred measures of oil... fifty — batos (bath — a Hebrew liquid measure, approx. 8-9 gallons / 30-35 liters; 100 baths ≈ 800+ gallons of olive oil, a large commercial quantity). The reduction from 100 to 50 is 50% — a massive forgiveness of debt. Dexai sou ta grammata kai kathisas tacheōs grapson pentēkonta — take your bill, sit down quickly, and write fifty — gramma (written document, bill — from graphō; the bond or promissory note that establishes the debt) must be rewritten by the debtor himself (not the manager), which may be important legally — the debtor re-writes the obligation and thus creates a new document.
7: hekaton korous sitou — a hundred kors of wheat — koros (cor — a Hebrew dry measure, approx. 10-12 bushels; 100 cors ≈ 1000-1200 bushels of wheat, a very large agricultural quantity). The reduction to 80 (20% less than oil's 50% reduction) may reflect different interest rates on the two commodities, or different profit margins the manager had taken. Various interpretations exist: (1) the debts represent illegal interest (usury) that the manager removes, legitimizing the reduction; (2) the manager forgoes his own commission, which was built into the inflated debt; (3) simple forgiveness to generate gratitude.
8: epēnesen ho kyrios ton oikonomon tēs adikias — the master commended the dishonest manager — the interpretive crux: who is ho kyrios (the master)? If the master of the parable, the commendation is for shrewdness in crisis management; if Jesus himself is speaking as kyrios, it is direct authorial commendation. Phronimōs epoiēsen — acted shrewdly — phronimōs (shrewdly, wisely — from phronimos = prudent, practical-minded; the "shrewd" word in the parable of the ten virgins, Matt 25:2-9) is the virtue of practical intelligence, not ethical virtue. Hoi huioi tou aiōnos toutou phronimōteroi... eisin — the sons of this age are shrewder than the sons of light — the comparison is pragmatic: worldly people apply more ingenuity to their material affairs than believers do to their eternal affairs.
9: poiēsate heautois philous ek tou mamōna tēs adikias — make friends for yourselves from the mammon of unrighteousness — mamōnas (Mammon — Aramaic loanword māmōnāʾ = wealth, money, property; appears only at Matt 6:24 and Luke 16:9, 11, 13 in the NT; not a proper name or deity in Second Temple Judaism but an Aramaic term for material wealth that Jesus uses as if it were a master, hence the personification). Tēs adikias (of unrighteousness — the genitive characterizes the nature of worldly wealth: it belongs to the unrighteous age and participates in systemic injustice). Skēnas aiōnious (eternal tents/dwellings — skēnē = tent; the eternal version echoes the heavenly dwelling).
10: ho pistos en elachistō kai en pollō pistos estin — the faithful in very little is also faithful in much — the faithfulness principle: pistos (faithful, trustworthy — from pisteō = trust, believe; pistos as an adjective of character = one who can be trusted) in the elachistō (least, smallest — superlative of mikros) predicts faithfulness in pollō (much). The logic is character-consistency: how one handles small matters reveals how one will handle large ones. The faithful-unfaithful antithesis repeats the manager's failure: he was unfaithful in small things (embezzlement) and would be unfaithful in large things (true wealth).
11: ei oun en tō adikō mamōna pistos ouk egenesthe — if then you have not been faithful in the unrighteous mammon — adikos mamōnas (unrighteous mammon = worldly wealth, the money of the current age) is the test case for faithfulness: handling money honestly reveals whether one can be trusted with to alēthinon (the true, genuine — from alētheia = truth; the "real" as opposed to the "transient"). The contrast is between adikos mamōnas (unrighteous mammon = money) and to alēthinon (the genuine wealth = spiritual/eternal goods). Money is the training ground for the eternal.
12: en tō allotriō pistos ouk egenesthe, to hymeteron tis hymin dōsei — if you have not been faithful with someone else's property, who will give you what is your own — allotrion (another's property, what belongs to another — from allos = other; what is alien to oneself) vs hymeteron (your own — the possessive pronoun): worldly wealth is not "ours" but belongs to another (God's world, administered for a time); the "our own" (to hymeteron) is what is genuinely ours in eternity. The faithful manager of another's property earns what will be truly his own.
13: oudeis oiketēs dunatai dusi kyriois douleuein — no household servant can serve two masters — oiketēs (household servant — from oikos = house; one who belongs to the household, a domestic slave; the domestic version of doulos; also Acts 10:7; Rom 14:4; 1 Pet 2:18) rather than doulos (slave) — the distinction emphasizes the household-allegiance dimension. ē gar ton hena misēsei kai ton heteron agapēsei — for he will hate the one and love the other — miseō/agapaō (hate/love) as comparative-preference antonyms: complete devotion to one master produces functional hatred of the other. Theō douleuein kai mamōna — serve God and Mammon — the personification of Mammon as a rival kyrios (master) makes wealth-love equivalent to idolatry.
14: ēkouon de tauta panta hoi Pharisaioi philargyroi ontes kai exemyktērizon auton — the Pharisees, who were money-lovers, heard all these things and sneered at him — philargyros (money-lover — from phileō = love + arguros = silver/money; the compound = lover of money; also 2 Tim 3:2) is Luke's diagnosis; the Pharisees' monetary devotion makes them the illustration of serving Mammon. Exemyktērizon (sneered at, turned up the nose at — from ek- + myktērizō = to turn up the nose, sneer; from myktēr = nostril; the nose-turning gesture of contempt; also Gal 6:7 where God is not mocked/myktērizetai).
15: hymeis este hoi dikaiountes heautous enōpion tōn anthrōpōn — you are the ones who justify yourselves before men — dikaioō (justify, declare righteous — from dikaios = just, righteous; the justification verb of Pauline theology, here used by Jesus for self-justification before human audiences). The Pharisees' dikaioō heautous (self-justification) before human observers (enōpion tōn anthrōpōn) is contrasted with God's knowledge of hearts. ho de theos ginōskei tas kardias hymōn — God knows your hearts — kardiognōstēs (heart-knower) is used for God in Acts 1:24 and 15:8; here the verb form: God's heart-knowledge penetrates self-presentation.
16: ho nomos kai hoi prophētai mechri Iōannou — the Law and the Prophets until John — the canonical phrase ho nomos kai hoi prophētai (the Law and the Prophets) = the OT as a whole; the temporal boundary is John the Baptist. Apo tote hē basileia tou theou euangelizetai — from then the kingdom of God is proclaimed — euangelizō (proclaim good news — the proclamation verb of Luke-Acts; also 4:18; 7:22; 9:6; 16:16). kai pas eis autēn biazetai — and everyone forces his way into it — biazetai (forces/is forced — from biazō = to use force; the verb can be middle [forces his way] or passive [is forced in]; the meaning is disputed: either eagerness/urgency of entry or violent opposition against the kingdom). The Matthew parallel (11:12: hē basileia tōn ouranōn biazetai) supports the violence/forcing reading.
17: eukopōteron de estin ton ouranon kai tēn gēn parelthein — it is easier for heaven and earth to pass away — eukopoteros (easier — comparative of eukopos = easy; from eu + kopos = good-effort; the easy word; "easier" appears also in the camel-needle saying 18:25). Parelthein (pass away, pass by — aorist of parerchomai = pass beside, go past; also used for Jesus's words not passing away, 21:33). Ē tou nomou mian keraian pesein — than for one stroke of the pen to fall from the Law — keraia (stroke, horn-like mark — the tiny decorative mark on a Hebrew letter; the smallest unit of the Torah text; also Matt 5:18 in the "not one iota or keraia" saying). The Torah's permanence is established by comparison to cosmic stability.
18: pas ho apolyōn tēn gynaika autou kai gamōn heteran moicheuei — everyone who divorces his wife and marries another commits adultery — apolyō (release, dismiss, divorce — the standard divorce verb; the formal release of a wife from the marriage bond under Jewish law). The saying appears here in Luke without the Matthean exception clause (parektos logou porneias = except for sexual immorality, Matt 5:32; 19:9). Luke's absolute form of the saying is a Greek legal formulation without the Jewish exception-clause debate context; it may address a different situation (Gentile divorce practice). Kai ho apolelumenēn apo andros gamōn moicheuei — and the man who marries a woman divorced from a husband commits adultery — the second clause extends the prohibition to the man who marries the divorced woman.
19: anthrōpos de tis ēn plousios kai enedidysketo porphyran kai bussinon — there was a rich man who was dressed in purple and fine linen — plousios (rich — from ploutos = wealth; the rich-man word throughout Luke as the economic counterpart to ptōchos = poor). Porphyran (purple — the color of wealth and royalty; porphyra = the Tyrian purple dye extracted from sea snails at enormous expense; 10,000 snails for one gram of dye; the fabric therefore signaled extreme wealth) + bussinon (fine linen — from byssos = Egyptian fine linen, imported luxury fabric; also used for the Jerusalem temple curtain and the clothing of the righteous in Rev 19:8). Euphrainomenos kath hēmeran lampros — feasting sumptuously every day — the daily (kath hēmeran) sumptuousness (lampros = brilliantly, magnificently) characterizes excess rather than occasional celebration.
20: ptōchos de tis onomati Lazaros ebeblēto pros ton pylōna autou — a poor man named Lazarus was laid at his gate — Lazaros (the Hellenized form of Hebrew El-āzār = God helps) is the only named character in any of Jesus's parables — the detail is significant; the rich man is unnamed throughout. Ebeblēto (pluperfect passive of ballō = throw; the pluperfect indicates a state already established: "was lying placed/thrown") at the gate (pylōn = gate, gateway — the grand entrance of a wealthy estate). The begging-posture (ptōchos = beggar, destitute — from ptōssō = crouch/cower; the destitute-beggar word, not merely economically poor) at the gate is the maximum social contrast to the purple-clad feaster within.
21: epithymōn chortasthēnai apo tōn piptontōn apo tēs trapezēs tou plousiou — longing to be filled from what fell from the rich man's table — epithumeō (long for earnestly — the strong desire verb; also 15:16 for the prodigal's longing for pig-pods; 22:15 for Jesus's Passover longing) + chortasthēnai (to be filled/satisfied — the animal-satiety word; also 15:16; the miraculous feeding verb). All kai hoi kynes erchomenoi apeleichon ta helkē autou — even the dogs were coming and licking his sores — helkos (sore, wound, ulcer) + apoleichō (lick off — compound of apo- + leichō = lick). The dog-licking is ambiguous in the Greek: torment (unclean animals touching his wounds) or comfort (the only contact he receives).
22: egeneto de apothanein ton ptōchon kai apenechthēnai auton hypo tōn angelōn — the poor man died and was carried by the angels — apenechthēnai (to be carried away — aorist passive infinitive of apopherō = carry away, bring away; the angelic transport verb) contrasts with the rich man's burial (etaphē = was buried). No burial is mentioned for Lazarus — in antiquity, burial was the transition to the honored afterlife; Lazarus's burial is omitted, foregrounded by angelic transport instead. eis ton kolpon Abraam — into Abraham's bosom/side — kolpos (bosom, chest, side — also John 1:18 for the Son "in the Father's bosom"; 13:23 for the Beloved Disciple reclining in Jesus's bosom at the Last Supper): the closest possible position to the covenant patriarch.
23: en tō hadē eparas tous ophthalmous autou — in Hades, lifting up his eyes — hadēs (Hades — the Greek underworld/realm of the dead; adopted into Jewish afterlife vocabulary; in the LXX it translates sheol; in the NT, distinct from gehenna as the permanent place of destruction; Hades is the intermediate state or general underworld). Eparas tous ophthalmous (lifting up his eyes — the gesture of deliberate upward vision, also 6:20; 18:13) from below to see Abraham above. hora Abraam apo makrothen kai Lazaron en tois kolpois autou — he sees Abraham far away and Lazarus in his bosom — the spatial reversal is complete: the one who was at the gate below is now in the place of honor above; the rich man who feasted daily is now in torment below.
24: pater Abraam, eleēson me — Father Abraham, have mercy on me — eleēson me (have mercy on me — aorist imperative of eleaō = show mercy/compassion; the same cry of the blind men and the Syrophoenician woman throughout the Gospels; the kyrie eleison of liturgical tradition) addressed to pater Abraam (Father Abraham — the Abrahamic-family claim). Pempson Lazaron — send Lazarus — the rich man still addresses Lazarus only through Abraham; he treats Lazarus as an errand-runner even in the afterlife, suggesting his social perception has not changed. Hina bapsē to akron tou daktylou autou hydatos kai katapsyxē tēn glōssan mou — to dip the tip of his finger in water and cool my tongue.
25: teknon, mnēsthēti hoti apelabas ta agatha sou en tē zōē sou — child, remember that you received your good things in your lifetime — teknon (child — Abraham's address to the rich man is the same affectionate word the father used for the older son in 15:31; the warmth of the address sharpens the severity of the content). Apelabas (you received in full — aorist of apolambanō = receive back, receive in full; the prefix apo- implies complete receipt, payment-in-full). The reversal logic: goods received fully in this life; now the opposite. Kai Lazaros homoiōs ta kaka nun de hōde parakaleitai — Lazarus likewise received bad things, but now here he is comforted — parakaleō (be comforted, be encouraged — the Paraclete verb in its comfort function).
26: en pasi toutois metaxy hēmōn kai hymōn chasma mega estēriktai — in all this, between us and you a great chasm has been fixed — chasma (chasm, gulf — from chainō = yawn, gape; a gaping void; only NT occurrence; the specific word for an unbridgeable geological void) + mega (great) + estēriktai (has been fixed/established — perfect passive of stērizō = set firm, establish; the same verb for Jesus setting his face toward Jerusalem at 9:51). The permanence (stērizō perfect) of the separation is its defining characteristic: the chasm is not temporary but set in place once death has established the final state.
27: erōtō se oun, pater, hina pempsēs auton eis ton oikon tou patros mou — I beg you then, father, send him to my father's house — the rich man's second request shifts from personal relief to family warning; the petition is still through Abraham (pempsēs auton = send him), still treating Lazarus as a servant. Erōtō (I ask, I beg — from erōtaō; formal request verb) used for a serious petition. The family concern is genuine but comes too late and through the wrong channel; he cannot direct Lazarus any more than he could ignore him at his gate.
28: echō gar pente adelphous, hopōs diamartyretai autois — for I have five brothers, so that he may warn them — diamartyromai (testify solemnly, warn emphatically — from dia- + martys = witness through; intensive testimony verb; also Acts 2:40; 8:25; 18:5; 20:23-24; 28:23) — the request is for solemn warning testimony to prevent the brothers from arriving at the same place of torment. The rich man's concern for others emerges only in extremis, and even here it is framed in terms of his own family rather than universal compassion.
29: echousin Mōysea kai tous prophētas — they have Moses and the Prophets — the canonical phrase reappears (v.16): the OT witness (Torah + Prophets) as already sufficient. Akousatōsan autōn — let them listen to them — akouō (hear, listen, obey — the comprehensive hearing verb of Deuteronomy: shema = hear, obey) in the imperative. Abraham's point is that the Scriptures already contain everything necessary for repentance; the rich man's brothers have what is needed — they need only listen. The parable's argument is that the problem is not insufficient evidence but unwillingness to hear what is already available.
30: ouchi, pater Abraam, all ean tis apo nekrōn poreuthē pros autous, metanoēsousin — no, Father Abraham, but if someone from the dead goes to them they will repent — metanoeō (repent, change mind — from meta- + noeō = change thinking; turn around in mind and direction; the repentance verb) as the goal of the desired apparition. The rich man's request for a post-mortem missionary implies that Scripture is insufficient — a direct contradiction of Abraham's position. The irony of the parable is prospective: apo nekrōn (from the dead) anticipates Jesus's own resurrection, which the religious leaders will reject despite being confronted with it.
31: ei Mōyseōs kai tōn prophētōn ouk akouousin, oude ean tis ek nekrōn anastē peisthēsontai — if they do not hear Moses and the Prophets, neither will they be convinced if someone rises from the dead — anastē (rises — aorist active subjunctive of anistēmi = rise, stand up; the resurrection verb) from the dead (ek nekrōn) is the precise phrase for Jesus's resurrection throughout Luke-Acts (24:46; Acts 10:41; 17:3). Peisthēsontai (be convinced, be persuaded — future passive of peithō = persuade) — Abraham's final word predicts the historical outcome: the resurrection of Jesus will not persuade those who have already refused Moses and the Prophets (Luke 20:1-8; Acts 4:1-22).
